advisory.checklist_item: - [ ] Treat an absent/unspecified `language` as NON-Python (or require an explicit `python` language) before attempting environment resolution, so a non-Python reference can never resolve against a same-named installed Python module.
evidence: resolve.py:654: `if language is not None and language.strip().lower() not in ("", "python"):` — a None or empty language is treated as Python.
resolve.py:485-488: the member gate calls `_refute_via_environment(ref)` with no prior language check.
resolve.py:549: the `unsupported_lang` abstain only fires when `isinstance(lang, str) and lang` — an omitted language never trips it and flows to environment resolution.

ABSENCE: no test in tests/unit/grounding/test_environment_resolution.py exercises a reference with the `language` field omitted; the resolving cases all pass `"language": "python"` and the negative case passes `language="rust"`.
advisory.finding: Environment resolution defaults an absent/empty `language` to Python, so a non-Python symbol/member reference with no declared language whose name collides with an installed Python module (or module.attr) is wrongly REFUTED — a false absence-disproof that undermines the 0-false-refute property.
scenarios: A JS/TS/Go reference like `crypto.randomBytes` or a member `path.join` with no declared language where a same-named Python module/attr is installed in site-packages.
A bare symbol name matching a stdlib/third-party top-level module (e.g. `json`, `time`) originating from a non-Python file with language unspecified.
advisory.suggested_fix: In `resolve_in_environment`, only proceed when `language` is explicitly `"python"` (return None for None/empty), OR have `_refute_via_environment` pass through only when `ref.get("language")` is python; add a test for an omitted-language member colliding with an installed module.

advisory.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm executing installed-package `__init__` during member grounding is acceptable, and consider constraining member resolution to spec-only checks or documenting the execution boundary in the oracle contract.
evidence: resolve.py:703: `mod = importlib.import_module(module)` inside `_attribute_exists`.
resolve.py:648-651 docstring: `find_spec never executes the target module; an attribute bind imports the module (running its package __init__) but only when an attribute is actually requested.`
oracle.py:120-121: the facade contract states it `NEVER raises on a resolution failure (fail-open through the lanes)` — but import-time side effects (not just raises) are a new class of behavior.
advisory.finding: The T1 grounding lane now executes arbitrary installed-module code: `_attribute_exists` calls `importlib.import_module(module)`, running the target package's `__init__` (and any import-time side effects) during member resolution, where the lane was previously purely static (ctags + non-executing find_spec).
scenarios: A member reference `some_pkg.attr` from an untrusted plan/diff triggers importing `some_pkg`, executing its module-level code as a side effect of grounding.
A package whose import is slow or has heavy import-time work is imported synchronously during refutation of every member reference naming it.

advisory.checklist_item: - [ ] Add/extend a runner or invariant test asserting the finder's assembled tool set includes `resolve_symbol` (grounding_tools wired in).
evidence: runner.py:239-244 `tools = (pai_tools.filesystem_tools(cfg.repo_path) + pai_tools.grounding_tools(cfg.repo_path) + pai_tools.rebar_tools(...))`
test_agent_build_invariants.py:34-40 `test_expected_tools_offered` asserts only filesystem + rebar tool names; docstring says it catches 'a #5177 hook-strips-a-tool regression'

ABSENCE: search of tests/ for `grounding_tools` returns only test_resolve_symbol_tool.py (which tests the factory in isolation); no test exercises the runner's assembled tool set, so a regression dropping grounding_tools from runner assembly is undetected
advisory.finding: The runner now assembles `grounding_tools` into the finder tool set (runner.py:239-244) but no test asserts the runner-offered tools include `resolve_symbol`; the existing tool-inventory invariant `test_expected_tools_offered` was not updated to cover the new tool.
scenarios: A refactor of the runner tool-assembly block silently drops grounding_tools; the fix (resolve_symbol available to the finder) regresses with no failing test
advisory.suggested_fix: Extend test_expected_tools_offered (or add a runner test) to assert `resolve_symbol` appears in the runner-assembled tool set, e.g. include `pai_tools.grounding_tools('.')` names in the expected inventory.

advisory.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a `resolve_symbol` test that resolves a repo-local module and asserts the output reports `[repo-local, ...]`.
evidence: pai_tools.py:180-181 `inside = _within_root(...)` / `scope = 'repo-local' if inside else 'third-party/stdlib (site-packages)'`
test_resolve_symbol_tool.py:33-42 only assert `'third-party/stdlib' in out` for pydantic/BaseModel
ABSENCE: no test passes a repo-local module (origin inside repo_path) to assert an `EXISTS: ... [repo-local, ...]` output
advisory.finding: The `resolve_symbol` tool's 'repo-local' scope branch (and the `_within_root` origin gating) is never exercised — all tool tests resolve third-party pydantic, hitting only the 'third-party/stdlib (site-packages)' output.
scenarios: A regression inverts or breaks the repo-local vs third-party classification; the reviewer mislabels a repo-local symbol's scope with no failing test
advisory.suggested_fix: Add a test invoking `grounding_tools(<repo_root>)('rebar.grounding.resolve')` (or another module whose origin is inside the repo root) and assert the returned string contains `repo-local`.

advisory.checklist_item: - [ ] Constrain resolve_in_environment/_attribute_exists to an allowlist of resolvable modules (or a non-importing resolution strategy) so untrusted reference/tool input cannot trigger import-time side effects of arbitrary installed modules.
evidence: resolve.py:703 `mod = importlib.import_module(module)` inside `_attribute_exists`, invoked by `resolve_in_environment` for any candidate with an attr
resolve.py:687-690 the ONLY guard is `if not _IMPORTABLE_NAME_RE.match(module): return None` then `importlib.util.find_spec(module)` — an identifier-shape regex, not an allowlist of permitted modules
resolve.py:76-77 `_IMPORTABLE_NAME_RE = re.compile(r"^[A-Za-z_][A-Za-z0-9_]*(?:\.[A-Za-z_][A-Za-z0-9_]*)*$")` — permits ANY dotted identifier, e.g. `some_installed_pkg.submod`

pai_tools.py:155-168 `def resolve_symbol(name: str, module: str = "")` passes LLM-supplied `name`/`module` straight into `_resolve.resolve_in_environment(name, container=module or None, ...)` with no allowlist
runner.py: `grounding_tools(cfg.repo_path)` wires resolve_symbol into the finder agent's tool set, so the tool args are attacker/LLM-influenced content
advisory.finding: resolve_in_environment reaches importlib.import_module (and find_spec, which imports parent packages) with a module name derived from untrusted input (the reviewed diff/plan text via reference dicts, and the LLM-controlled resolve_symbol tool args), guarded only by an identifier-shape regex with NO module allowlist — so any syntactically-valid, importable module name causes that module's (and its parent packages') import-time code to EXECUTE as a side effect.
scenarios: A reviewed diff/plan names a dotted symbol whose module segment is an installed package with import-time side effects (network, filesystem writes, env mutation); the grounding oracle imports it while 'resolving' the reference

The finder LLM, steered by prompt-injected content in the diff under review, calls resolve_symbol('X', module='<installed side-effecting module>'), causing that module to be imported and its top-level code to run

advisory.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the 'no execution' guarantee and account for find_spec importing parent packages of dotted names (bound to top-level names, or use a non-importing loader).
evidence: resolve.py:684-685 docstring: `Uses :func:`importlib.util.find_spec`, which LOCATES (does not execute) the target module.`
resolve.py:648-650 docstring: `find_spec never executes the target module`
resolve.py:690 `spec = importlib.util.find_spec(module)` — for a dotted `module` like `pkg.sub`, CPython imports `pkg` (executing its __init__) to locate `sub`
resolve.py:667 candidates include `(nm, None)` where `nm` may be a dotted submodule path, so find_spec is called on dotted names
advisory.finding: The docstrings assert find_spec 'never executes the target module' / offers a 'no execution' guarantee, but importlib.util.find_spec IMPORTS the parent packages of a dotted module name (running their __init__.py) before locating the leaf — so _module_origin executes parent-package import-time code even on the supposedly execution-free path.
scenarios: A reference names `some_pkg.submodule` where `some_pkg/__init__.py` has import-time side effects; _module_origin's find_spec call imports `some_pkg` despite the 'no execution' claim

coaching.coaching: Threat-model the change to import-time code execution in environment resolution (trust boundary, input validation).
coaching.subject: import-time code execution in environment resolution
